question: An ecommerce application runs on a fleet of EC2 instances that write order records directly to a backend processing service. During flash sales the backend cannot keep up and orders are lost when it is overwhelmed. Which change MOST improves resilience to these traffic spikes with the least operational overhead?
options: Move the processing service behind an Application Load Balancer
Vertically scale the processing servers to the largest available instance type
Place an Amazon SQS queue between the web tier and the processing tier
Increase the number of processing servers and leave them running at peak count permanently
explanation:
An SQS queue decouples producers from consumers. The queue durably absorbs bursts and the processing tier pulls messages at its own pace, so spikes no longer cause lost orders and you can scale consumers on queue depth. Vertical scaling has a ceiling and still fails at a higher spike, and it is not elastic. An Application Load Balancer distributes requests but does not buffer work, so an overwhelmed backend still drops requests. Permanently running peak capacity wastes money during normal load and still has no buffer if demand exceeds that fixed capacity.

Sources: [Amazon SQS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSSimpleQueueService/latest/SQSDeveloperGuide/welcome.html)

question: A photo sharing platform must notify several independent systems (a thumbnail generator, a search indexer, and an audit logger) every time a new image is uploaded. Each system must receive every event independently. Which approach is MOST suitable?
options: Publish each upload event to an Amazon SNS topic and subscribe each system to it
Store events in Amazon DynamoDB and have each system scan the table periodically
Have the uploader call each system synchronously over HTTP
Write each event to a single Amazon SQS queue shared by all three systems
explanation:
SNS fan-out publishes one message to a topic and delivers a copy to every subscriber, so all three systems receive every event independently. Subscribing each system through its own SQS queue adds durability and lets each process at its own pace. A single shared standard SQS queue is wrong because each message is delivered to only one consumer, so the systems would compete for messages instead of all receiving them. Synchronous HTTP couples the uploader to every downstream system and fails if any one is down. Polling DynamoDB with periodic scans adds latency and cost and is inefficient.

Sources: [Amazon SNS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sns/latest/dg/welcome.html), [Common SNS scenarios (fan-out)](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sns/latest/dg/sns-common-scenarios.html)

question: A payments service must process transactions for each customer in the exact order they were submitted, with no duplicates, while still processing different customers in parallel. Which configuration meets this requirement?
options: An Amazon SQS standard queue with long polling enabled
An Amazon SQS FIFO queue using the customer ID as the message group ID
An Amazon Kinesis data stream with a single shard
An Amazon SNS standard topic with multiple subscribers
explanation:
SQS FIFO queues guarantee ordering and exactly-once processing within a message group. Using the customer ID as the message group ID preserves order per customer while letting different customers be processed in parallel. A standard queue gives best-effort ordering and at-least-once delivery, so duplicates and reordering are possible. An SNS standard topic does not guarantee ordering. A single-shard Kinesis stream does order records, but it serializes everything through one shard, removing the per-customer parallelism and adding more operational weight than FIFO message groups.

Sources: [Amazon SQS FIFO queues](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSSimpleQueueService/latest/SQSDeveloperGuide/FIFO-queues.html)

question: A company runs its web tier on a single large Amazon EC2 instance in one Availability Zone. It wants the web tier to stay available if an instance or an entire Availability Zone fails, and to scale with demand. Which TWO actions together achieve this? (Select TWO.)
options: Enable termination protection on the instance
Launch the instances in an Auto Scaling group that spans at least two Availability Zones
Place the instances behind an Application Load Balancer that targets multiple Availability Zones
Replace the instance with a larger instance type in the same Availability Zone
Create an Amazon Machine Image and store it for manual recovery
explanation:
An Auto Scaling group across two or more Availability Zones replaces failed instances automatically and adds or removes capacity with demand, providing both fault tolerance and elasticity. An Application Load Balancer that spans multiple Availability Zones spreads traffic across healthy targets and stops routing to unhealthy ones, so losing an instance or an Availability Zone does not take the tier down. A larger single instance is still a single point of failure in one Availability Zone. Storing an AMI helps rebuild but is manual and provides no automatic availability. Termination protection only prevents accidental termination and does nothing for Availability Zone failure or scaling.

Sources: [Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/ec2/userguide/what-is-amazon-ec2-auto-scaling.html), [Elastic Load Balancing](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/userguide/what-is-load-balancing.html)

question: A relational database on Amazon RDS must remain available with minimal downtime if the database instance or its Availability Zone fails, and the team does not want to manage replication manually. Which option meets this requirement MOST directly?
options: Enable a Multi-AZ deployment for the RDS instance
Create a read replica in the same Availability Zone
Increase the instance size to provide more memory
Take automated snapshots every hour
explanation:
RDS Multi-AZ keeps a synchronous standby replica in a different Availability Zone and fails over automatically, which is purpose built for high availability with minimal downtime and no manual replication. A read replica is for scaling reads and uses asynchronous replication; promoting it on failure is manual and can lose data, and a same-Availability-Zone replica does not protect against an Availability Zone failure. Hourly snapshots imply data loss of up to an hour and a slow restore, not minimal downtime. A larger instance improves performance, not availability.

Sources: [RDS Multi-AZ](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/UserGuide/Concepts.MultiAZ.html), [RDS read replicas](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/UserGuide/USER_ReadRepl.html)

question: A company needs a disaster recovery plan in a second AWS Region for a business critical application. The recovery time objective is a few minutes and the recovery point objective is seconds. The business wants to avoid paying to run two identical full size production environments at all times. Which disaster recovery strategy best fits?
options: Backup and restore: restore from backups in the second Region when disaster strikes
Multi-site active/active with full production capacity in both Regions
Pilot light with all application servers switched off until needed
Warm standby: run a scaled down but always running copy in the second Region and scale it up on failover
explanation:
Warm standby keeps a smaller, always running copy of the workload in the recovery Region with continuous data replication, so it can take traffic almost immediately and then scale to full capacity. That meets a recovery time objective of minutes and a recovery point objective of seconds without paying for two full environments. Backup and restore has recovery times measured in hours and a larger recovery point. Pilot light keeps data replicating but application servers are off, so it needs time to start and scale and usually misses a few-minute recovery time objective. Multi-site active/active meets the lowest objectives but runs full capacity in both Regions, which is the cost the business wants to avoid.

Sources: [Disaster recovery options in the cloud (AWS whitepaper)](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/whitepapers/latest/disaster-recovery-workloads-on-aws/disaster-recovery-options-in-the-cloud.html)

question: A web application stores user session data in the memory of each EC2 instance. When Auto Scaling adds or removes instances, users are unexpectedly logged out. What is the best way to fix this while keeping the application able to scale horizontally?
options: Enable sticky sessions on the load balancer and stop using Auto Scaling
Store session state in an external shared store such as Amazon ElastiCache or Amazon DynamoDB
Increase the instance size so sessions are not lost
Write session data to the local disk of each instance
explanation:
Externalizing session state to a shared store such as ElastiCache for Redis or DynamoDB makes the instances stateless, so any instance can serve any user and scaling in or out never loses sessions. Sticky sessions pin a user to one instance, but when Auto Scaling terminates that instance the session is still lost, and disabling Auto Scaling sacrifices elasticity. A larger instance does not change the fact that in-memory state disappears when the instance is removed. Local disk has the same problem as memory because it is tied to a single instance.

Sources: [Amazon ElastiCache](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonElastiCache/latest/dg/WhatIs.html), [Caching strategies](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonElastiCache/latest/red-ug/Strategies.html)

question: A media company serves large static assets such as images and videos from an Amazon S3 bucket to a global audience. Users far from the bucket Region report slow downloads. Which solution improves performance MOST cost effectively with the least operational overhead?
options: Host the assets on a fleet of EC2 instances behind a load balancer in each Region
Serve the assets through Amazon CloudFront with the S3 bucket as the origin
Change the S3 bucket storage class to S3 Standard-IA
Enable S3 Cross-Region Replication to a bucket on every continent and route users manually
explanation:
CloudFront is a content delivery network that caches static assets at edge locations close to users, reducing latency and offloading the origin, with very little operational overhead and lower data transfer cost from the origin. Replicating to buckets on every continent and routing users manually is operationally heavy and expensive compared with a managed CDN. Running EC2 fleets to serve static files rebuilds a CDN at higher cost and complexity. Changing the storage class to Standard-IA affects cost and adds retrieval fees for frequently accessed content; it does not address global latency.

Sources: [Amazon CloudFront](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudFront/latest/DeveloperGuide/Introduction.html), [S3 storage classes](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/storage-class-intro.html)

question: A company runs a global multiplayer game whose API uses long lived TCP and UDP connections. The traffic is dynamic and not cacheable. The company wants to improve global performance and provide static anycast IP addresses for the endpoints. Which service is the best fit?
options: AWS Global Accelerator
Amazon CloudFront
Amazon API Gateway edge optimized endpoints
Amazon Route 53 latency based routing on its own
explanation:
Global Accelerator routes traffic over the AWS global network from the nearest edge to the application, improving performance for non-cacheable TCP and UDP traffic, and it provides static anycast IP addresses, which suits gaming and other dynamic workloads. CloudFront is optimized for cacheable HTTP and HTTPS content and does not serve arbitrary UDP, so it does not fit a non-cacheable TCP/UDP protocol. Route 53 latency routing only influences DNS resolution; it does not move packets onto the AWS backbone or provide static anycast IPs. API Gateway is for HTTP, REST, and WebSocket APIs, not arbitrary TCP/UDP game traffic.

Sources: [AWS Global Accelerator](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/global-accelerator/latest/dg/what-is-global-accelerator.html), [Amazon CloudFront](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudFront/latest/DeveloperGuide/Introduction.html)

question: A read heavy web application repeatedly runs the same expensive query against an Amazon RDS database, and the database CPU is saturated. The team wants to reduce latency and offload the database with minimal application change. What should they add?
options: A second Multi-AZ standby instance
A larger storage volume with more provisioned IOPS
An in-memory cache such as Amazon ElastiCache in front of the database
AWS Global Accelerator in front of the database
explanation:
ElastiCache stores the results of frequent, expensive queries in memory, so repeated reads are served with sub-millisecond latency and never reach the database, directly reducing database CPU. A Multi-AZ standby is for failover and does not serve read traffic, so it cannot offload reads. More IOPS helps disk bound workloads but does not stop the repeated identical query execution that is saturating CPU. Global Accelerator improves the network path to internet facing endpoints and is not a database caching layer.

Sources: [Amazon ElastiCache](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonElastiCache/latest/dg/WhatIs.html), [Caching strategies](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonElastiCache/latest/red-ug/Strategies.html)

question: An analytics dashboard generates a growing volume of read only queries against a production Amazon RDS database, slowing down the primary. Writes are moderate. How can the team scale read capacity while keeping the primary responsive?
options: Add one or more RDS read replicas and direct dashboard read queries to them
Put an Application Load Balancer in front of the database
Enable Multi-AZ so the standby can serve the dashboard queries
Convert the database to a single larger instance and route all traffic to it
explanation:
RDS read replicas provide additional read only endpoints that asynchronously replicate from the primary. Pointing the dashboard at replicas offloads read traffic and keeps the primary free for writes and latency sensitive reads. Multi-AZ standby instances do not serve read traffic; they exist only for failover. Vertical scaling has a ceiling and still funnels all reads and writes through one instance. An Application Load Balancer balances HTTP application traffic, not SQL database connections, and adds no read capacity.

Sources: [RDS read replicas](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/UserGuide/USER_ReadRepl.html), [RDS Multi-AZ](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/UserGuide/Concepts.MultiAZ.html)

question: A self managed transactional database runs on an EC2 instance and requires consistent, high IOPS with low latency on a volume that must persist independently of the instance lifecycle. Which Amazon EBS volume type is the best fit?
options: Instance store volumes
Throughput Optimized HDD (st1)
Provisioned IOPS SSD (io2 or io2 Block Express)
Cold HDD (sc1)
explanation:
Provisioned IOPS SSD volumes (io2 or io2 Block Express) let you specify a consistent, high IOPS level with low latency, which is exactly what a latency sensitive transactional database needs, and EBS volumes persist independently of the instance lifecycle. st1 is a throughput optimized HDD for large sequential workloads such as big data and log processing, not high random IOPS. sc1 is the lowest cost HDD for infrequently accessed data and has the lowest performance. Instance store is physically attached and ephemeral, so data is lost when the instance stops or fails, which fails the persistence requirement.

Sources: [Amazon EBS volume types](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/ebs-volume-types.html)

question: A company must ingest a high volume real time clickstream, allow multiple independent consumers to process the same records, and be able to replay records from the last 24 hours. Which service best meets these needs?
options: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams
An Amazon SQS standard queue
Amazon Data Firehose delivering directly to Amazon S3
An Amazon SNS topic
explanation:
Kinesis Data Streams retains records for a configurable period (24 hours by default and extendable), supports multiple independent consumers reading the same stream, and allows replay by re-reading from an earlier position, which fits real time analytics with several consumers. Data Firehose is a fully managed delivery stream that buffers and loads data into destinations such as S3 or Redshift, but it does not retain data for arbitrary replay or support multiple independent stream consumers in the same way. An SQS standard queue deletes each message after one consumer processes it and supports neither replay nor multiple independent readers of the same message. SNS pushes messages to subscribers without retention or replay.

Sources: [Amazon Kinesis Data Streams](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/streams/latest/dev/introduction.html), [Amazon Data Firehose](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/firehose/latest/dev/what-is-this-service.html)

question: An application running on Amazon EC2 needs to read objects from an Amazon S3 bucket. What is the most secure way to grant this access?
options: Embed the access keys directly in the application code
Attach an IAM role (instance profile) with an S3 read policy to the EC2 instance
Make the S3 bucket public and access it over the internet
Store an IAM user access key and secret key in the application configuration file
explanation:
An IAM role attached to the instance supplies temporary, automatically rotated credentials through the instance metadata service, so no long lived secrets are stored on the instance. This is the AWS best practice. Storing access keys in a configuration file or embedding them in code creates long lived static credentials that can leak and must be rotated manually. Making the bucket public exposes the data to everyone and still does not scope access to just the application.

Sources: [IAM roles](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles.html), [Using an IAM role with EC2](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_use_switch-role-ec2.html)

question: Account A must allow an application in Account B to access resources in Account A without creating long lived credentials for Account B. Which approach follows AWS best practice?
options: Recreate all of Account B's users as IAM users inside Account A
Make the resources in Account A public so Account B can reach them
Create an IAM role in Account A that trusts Account B, and have Account B assume it with AWS STS
Create an IAM user in Account A and share its access keys with Account B
explanation:
A cross-account IAM role in Account A with a trust policy for Account B lets Account B call AWS STS AssumeRole to obtain temporary credentials, giving scoped, auditable access with no shared long lived secrets. Sharing access keys creates static credentials that are hard to rotate and audit. Making resources public removes access control entirely. Duplicating Account B's users inside Account A is unmanageable and defeats the purpose of cross-account roles.

Sources: [Cross-account access with IAM roles](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/tutorial_cross-account-with-roles.html), [IAM roles](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles.html)

question: An architect needs to allow inbound HTTPS only to specific EC2 instances, and the rule must be stateful so that response traffic is automatically allowed. Which control should be used?
options: An Amazon S3 bucket policy
An IAM policy attached to the instances
A network ACL on the subnet, allowing inbound TCP 443
A security group attached to the instances, allowing inbound TCP 443
explanation:
Security groups operate at the instance level and are stateful, so when inbound HTTPS is allowed, the return traffic is automatically permitted regardless of outbound rules. A network ACL is stateless and operates at the subnet level, so you must add matching inbound and outbound rules for ephemeral ports, and it applies to the entire subnet rather than specific instances. An IAM policy controls API permissions, not network traffic. An S3 bucket policy controls access to S3 objects and is unrelated to EC2 network filtering.

Sources: [Security groups](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-security-groups.html), [Network ACLs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-network-acls.html)

question: A team needs to store database credentials securely and automatically rotate them on a schedule with minimal custom code. Which service is the best fit?
options: AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store standard parameters
AWS Secrets Manager
An encrypted object stored in Amazon S3
An environment variable on each server
explanation:
Secrets Manager is built to store secrets and provides built in automatic rotation, including managed rotation for supported databases, with minimal custom code, plus fine grained access control and auditing. Parameter Store standard parameters can store secrets with KMS encryption but do not provide native scheduled rotation without you building it yourself with Lambda, so it needs more effort here. An encrypted S3 object can hold the value but offers no rotation or secret lifecycle features. Environment variables are static, visible to anyone who can inspect the process, and are not rotated.

Sources: [Rotating secrets in AWS Secrets Manager](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/secretsmanager/latest/userguide/rotating-secrets.html), [SSM Parameter Store](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/systems-manager-parameter-store.html)

question: A public web application behind an Application Load Balancer is receiving SQL injection and cross-site scripting attempts. The team wants to filter these malicious HTTP requests before they reach the application. Which service should they use?
options: AWS Shield Standard on its own
AWS WAF with managed rule groups on the Application Load Balancer
Amazon GuardDuty
A network ACL blocking the attacker IP ranges
explanation:
AWS WAF inspects HTTP and HTTPS requests at the application layer and can block common exploits such as SQL injection and cross-site scripting, including through AWS managed rule groups, and it attaches to an Application Load Balancer, CloudFront, or API Gateway. Shield Standard protects against common network and transport layer DDoS attacks but does not inspect application layer request content. A network ACL filters by IP and port and cannot understand HTTP payloads, so it cannot reliably stop injection patterns. GuardDuty detects and alerts on suspicious activity but does not inline block web requests.

Sources: [AWS WAF](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/what-is-aws-waf.html)

question: A company must encrypt sensitive data both in transit and at rest for an Amazon S3 backed web application, and it needs an audit trail of when the encryption keys are used. Which TWO actions meet these requirements? (Select TWO.)
options: Use server side encryption with Amazon S3 managed keys (SSE-S3) to obtain key usage audit logs
Use server side encryption with AWS KMS keys (SSE-KMS) for objects at rest
Store the data unencrypted but restrict the bucket with an IAM policy
Rely on the default network isolation of the VPC instead of using TLS
Serve the application over HTTPS using a TLS certificate from AWS Certificate Manager
explanation:
SSE-KMS encrypts objects at rest with KMS keys and logs every key use in AWS CloudTrail, satisfying both encryption at rest and the audit trail requirement. Serving the application over HTTPS with an ACM certificate encrypts data in transit. SSE-S3 also encrypts at rest, but its keys are fully managed by S3 and it does not provide the per request key usage audit trail that KMS gives, so it does not meet the auditing requirement. Relying on VPC isolation does not encrypt data in transit. Restricting the bucket with an IAM policy controls access but leaves the data unencrypted, failing the at rest requirement.

Sources: [SSE-KMS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingKMSEncryption.html), [SSE-S3](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingServerSideEncryption.html), [AWS Certificate Manager](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/acm/latest/userguide/acm-overview.html)

question: A company stores application logs in Amazon S3. Logs are accessed frequently for 30 days, rarely for the next 90 days, and almost never afterward, but they must be retained for seven years for compliance. Which approach minimizes storage cost with the least ongoing effort?
options: Configure an S3 Lifecycle policy that transitions objects to S3 Standard-IA, then to an S3 Glacier class, and expires them after seven years
Keep all logs in S3 Standard for the full seven years
Move all logs to S3 Glacier Deep Archive immediately on upload
Manually copy older logs to a cheaper bucket each month
explanation:
An S3 Lifecycle policy automatically transitions objects to colder, cheaper storage classes as they age, for example Standard to Standard-IA after 30 days and then to a Glacier class, and can expire them after seven years, matching the access pattern with no ongoing manual work. Manually copying logs every month is error prone operational overhead. Keeping everything in S3 Standard ignores the falling access frequency and is the most expensive option. Moving logs straight to Deep Archive on upload breaks the need for frequent access in the first 30 days, because Deep Archive retrieval takes hours and has a long minimum storage duration.

Sources: [S3 Lifecycle management](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/object-lifecycle-mgmt.html), [S3 storage classes](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/storage-class-intro.html)

question: A data lake on Amazon S3 holds objects whose access patterns are unpredictable and change over time. The company wants automatic cost optimization without retrieval fees and without having to analyze access patterns itself. Which storage class is the best choice?
options: S3 Intelligent-Tiering
S3 One Zone-IA
S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval
S3 Standard-IA
explanation:
S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves each object between access tiers based on its changing usage for a small monitoring fee and charges no retrieval fees, which is ideal when access patterns are unknown or unpredictable. Standard-IA assumes infrequent access and charges per GB retrieval fees, so unexpected reads become expensive. One Zone-IA is cheaper but stores data in a single Availability Zone, reducing resilience, and still has retrieval fees. Glacier Flexible Retrieval is for archive data with retrieval times of minutes to hours, not for data that might be read at any moment.

Sources: [S3 Intelligent-Tiering](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/intelligent-tiering.html), [S3 storage classes](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/storage-class-intro.html)

question: A nightly batch job runs on a fleet of EC2 worker instances that pull tasks from an Amazon SQS queue. The work is fault tolerant and can be retried, and the company wants to minimize cost. Which TWO actions reduce cost while keeping the job reliable? (Select TWO.)
options: Run the worker fleet on Amazon EC2 Spot Instances
Run the workers on the largest On-Demand instances available at all times
Use an Auto Scaling group that scales in to zero when the queue is empty
Purchase Reserved Instances sized for peak nightly capacity that run 24 hours a day
Move the queue to Amazon SNS to lower messaging cost
explanation:
Spot Instances offer steep discounts compared with On-Demand and suit fault tolerant, retryable work that can tolerate occasional interruption, which is exactly this batch job. Scaling the Auto Scaling group in to zero when the queue is empty means you pay only while there is work to do. Reserved Instances sized for peak that run all day would pay for capacity that is idle outside the nightly window. Keeping the largest On-Demand instances running at all times is the most expensive option. Switching to SNS does not fit a worker pull model and does not reduce the compute cost, which dominates here.

Sources: [Amazon EC2 Spot Instances](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/using-spot-instances.html), [Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/ec2/userguide/what-is-amazon-ec2-auto-scaling.html)

question: A company runs a steady, predictable baseline of EC2 compute 24 hours a day and wants to lower cost in exchange for a one or three year usage commitment, while keeping flexibility across instance families and Regions. Which purchasing option is the best fit?
options: A Standard Reserved Instance locked to one instance type and Region
Compute Savings Plans
Spot Instances
On-Demand Instances
explanation:
Compute Savings Plans give a significant discount for a one or three year commitment to a steady amount of compute spend and apply automatically across instance families, sizes, and Regions, and to Lambda and Fargate, which suits a predictable always on baseline that still wants flexibility. On-Demand has no commitment and the highest per hour price, so it does not minimize cost for steady usage. Spot is cheapest but can be interrupted, which is unsuitable for an always on baseline. A Standard Reserved Instance locked to one type and Region gives a discount but less flexibility than a Compute Savings Plan.

Sources: [AWS Savings Plans](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/savingsplans/latest/userguide/what-is-savings-plans.html)

question: EC2 instances in a private subnet download large amounts of data from Amazon S3. The traffic currently flows through a NAT gateway, and the company wants to reduce the associated data processing and transfer costs. What should the architect implement?
options: A larger NAT gateway
An interface VPC endpoint used as a general internet gateway
A gateway VPC endpoint for Amazon S3
An internet gateway attached to the private subnet
explanation:
A gateway VPC endpoint for S3 routes traffic to S3 privately over the AWS network with no NAT gateway in the path and no charge for the endpoint itself, removing the NAT data processing and transfer costs for S3 traffic. A larger NAT gateway still incurs per GB processing charges and does not address the cost driver. Attaching an internet gateway to the private subnet would make it public, a security change, and still would not give the private low cost path that a gateway endpoint provides. Interface endpoints are billed per hour and per GB and are used to reach individual services privately; for S3 and DynamoDB the gateway endpoint is the free and appropriate choice and is not a general internet gateway.

Sources: [Gateway VPC endpoints](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/privatelink/gateway-endpoints.html), [VPC endpoints](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/privatelink/concepts.html)

question: A development database receives bursts of traffic during working hours and is almost idle overnight and on weekends. The team wants to pay for database capacity only when it is used, without managing instance sizing. Which option is the most cost effective?
options: A large provisioned Aurora instance running continuously
A provisioned Amazon RDS instance sized for peak load
A fleet of EC2 instances running a self managed database
Amazon Aurora Serverless v2, which scales capacity up and down automatically with load
explanation:
Aurora Serverless v2 automatically scales database capacity up and down based on demand, so you pay for capacity in fine grained increments and far less during idle nights and weekends, with no manual instance sizing. A large always on provisioned Aurora instance pays for peak capacity around the clock and wastes money while idle. A provisioned RDS instance sized for peak has the same idle waste problem. Running a self managed database on EC2 adds operational burden and still pays for running instances when idle.

Sources: [Aurora Serverless v2](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/AuroraUserGuide/aurora-serverless-v2.html), [DynamoDB capacity modes](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/HowItWorks.ReadWriteCapacityMode.html)
